one_line_takeaway: Introduces a retain/forget 'mix-coherence' generalization of Hessian coherence to prove tight linear-stability thresholds for when gradient-based unlearning converges (fails) vs. diverges (succeeds), and shows theoretically and empirically that low-SNR/high-memorization data is provably easier to unlearn than high-SNR/low-memorization data.
key_finding: The two synthetic experiments confirm the theory tightly: in the Hessian-construction experiment, both the divergence criterion (Theorem 3.6, solid line) and the convergence lower bound (Theorem 3.7, dashed line) closely track the true empirical convergence/divergence boundary in Figure 1, with tighter bounds for batch sizes >= 10. In the CNN signal-plus-noise experiment, regions of high memorization (low training loss, high test error) coincide with regions of high forget-set loss (Figure 2), i.e., memorized models are the ones that forget most easily under gradient-based unlearning. The CIFAR-10/ResNet-18 experiment (Table 1) shows forget-set loss growing much faster with more injected input noise: at step 450 the mean forget loss is 2.5029 (noise variance 0.1) vs 2.9324 (variance 0.3) vs 5.5811 (variance 0.5), and at step 300 it is 0.3888 vs 0.5626 vs 1.4868 respectively -- monotonically increasing with memorization strength (noise variance), consistent with the theoretical prediction that more-memorizing models are provably easier to unlearn.
core_contribution: The paper develops the first theoretical framework analyzing machine unlearning through asymptotic linear stability of the SGD-based unlearning update operator (simultaneous descent on a retain set and ascent on a forget set, following Kurmanji et al. 2023's update rule). The authors introduce a 'mix-Hessian' D and a generalized 'unlearning coherence' measure sigma that extends Dexter et al. (2024)'s single-dataset Hessian coherence to the two-set retain/forget setting, capturing within-retain, within-forget, and cross retain-forget curvature alignment. Using this machinery they prove: (1) Theorem 3.6, a divergence criterion showing unlearning escapes the original minimum (succeeds) when lambda_max(D) exceeds a threshold set by sigma, batch size B, learning rate eta, and forget/retain set sizes nf/nr; (2) Theorem 3.7, a matching convergence lower bound showing the divergence threshold is tight up to constants; (3) Theorem 3.9, an analysis of a two-layer ReLU CNN under a signal-plus-noise data model (adapted from Kou et al. 2023's benign-overfitting construction) proving that lower signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in training data yields lower coherence, and hence models that memorize more (low SNR) are provably easier to unlearn, while high-SNR/highly-aligned models resist unlearning.
threat_model: None in the adversarial-attacker sense. This is a pure optimization/statistical-learning-theory paper: the 'actors' are the model, its retain set, and its forget set under a benign gradient-based unlearning algorithm. There is no adversary, no attack, no defense evaluation, and no threat actor. Its relevance to AI security/privacy is indirect: machine unlearning itself is motivated by privacy/regulatory deletion requirements (e.g., GDPR-style 'right to be forgotten'), and the paper's analysis could inform when compliance-driven unlearning is or is not achievable via gradient-based methods -- but the paper itself makes no such compliance or adversarial claims, and reviewers should not impute a threat model beyond what is stated.
limitations: The paper's own text flags several restrictive assumptions relegated to an appendix (A.2) that were not resolved in the main text I reviewed: sample-wise gradients at w* are assumed to be exactly zero (i.e., all gradient variation comes from curvature, eq. 2), which is described as following prior linear-interpolation-regime work but is a simplifying idealization. The CNN theoretical result (Theorem 3.9) is stated only as an O(...) upper bound on coherence under a specific synthetic signal-plus-noise data model with a two-layer ReLU CNN -- it is unclear from the reviewed text how far this generalizes to deep/non-convolutional architectures or real data distributions beyond the CIFAR-10 sanity check. The CIFAR-10 experiment (Table 1) has large standard deviations relative to the means (e.g., at step 450, variance-0.5 forget loss is 5.5811 +/- 1.8397), indicating high run-to-run variance. The synthetic Hessian experiment explicitly constructs Hessians of a specific diagonal/rank-controlled form (Hi = m*e1*e1^T or m*e_{i-Q+1}*e_{i-Q+1}^T) to control sharpness, which is a stylized construction rather than Hessians arising from real trained networks.

datasets_used: Synthetic Hessian-construction dataset (nf=nr=50 samples, rank-1 diagonal Hessians parameterized by hyperparameter Q)
Synthetic signal-plus-noise dataset for the two-layer ReLU CNN (Definition 3.8: 50 samples, signal vector mu, Gaussian noise sigma=1.0, dimension d swept over [100, 1100])
CIFAR-10 (used with ResNet-18, forget set = 10% of training set, with injected Gaussian input noise of variance 0.1/0.3/0.5)
quantitative_results: Figure 1 (synthetic Hessian experiment): divergence criterion (Theorem 3.6) and convergence lower bound (Theorem 3.7) both closely track the true empirical convergence/divergence boundary, tighter for batch sizes >= 10, shown for learning rates eta=0.5 and eta=0.8

Table 1 (CIFAR-10/ResNet-18 forget-set loss, mean +/- std, mini-batch unlearning with alpha=0.3, lr=0.01): step 0 -- 0.0016+/-0.0005 (var 0.1), 0.0019+/-0.0004 (var 0.3), 0.0016+/-0.0004 (var 0.5); step 300 -- 0.3888+/-0.4347 (var 0.1), 0.5626+/-0.5598 (var 0.3), 1.4868+/-0.9849 (var 0.5); step 450 -- 2.5029+/-2.3457 (var 0.1), 2.9324+/-1.2882 (var 0.3), 5.5811+/-1.8397 (var 0.5) -- forget loss increases monotonically with injected-noise-induced memorization strength
baselines_compared: Naive negative-gradient unlearning (alpha=1, ascent-only on forget set) -- analyzed theoretically via the coherence framework, not run as a numeric experimental baseline in the reviewed pages
Random label perturbation unlearning -- analyzed theoretically (coherence-lowering effect), not run as a numeric experimental baseline in the reviewed pages
Min-Max/targeted-forget unlearning methods (projection-based, citing Tang & Khanna 2025; Fan et al. 2024) -- analyzed theoretically, not run as a numeric experimental baseline in the reviewed pages
